problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy): teams had to take an existing "green GDP" (GGDP) measure, model what would happen to global climate if every country adopted GGDP instead of GDP, weigh whether the switch is worthwhile, and analyze/recommend GGDP adoption for one chosen country (here Australia), ending with a one-page government letter. This O-award paper (Team 2311258, "'Green' GDP, Green planet") builds a five-task pipeline: define and compute GGDP for 46 countries, forecast its climate effect, argue feasibility via game theory + time-dimension benefit analysis, diagnose Australia with a PVAR industrial model, and deliver tailored policy.
models_used: GGDP accounting formula (Stjepanovic/Tomic/Skare 2017 method): GGDP = GDP - E_CO2*MPC - Waste*Elect*Pelect - GDP*NRR/100; defines RGG = GGDP/GDP as a 'greenness' ratio for 46 countries

Time-series + linear-fit forecasting of E_CO2, Waste, NRR and GDP from 2021-2040, with a policy impact factor theta=3% calibrated from real historical shocks (2007-08 financial crisis, 2014 US-China climate statement)

Three-level (multi-level) fuzzy comprehensive evaluation model for climate, with three indicators B1 temperature / B2 pollution / B3 ecosystem balance; membership functions built from 2020 quantiles (min + quartile matrix Q) of 46-country data; four rubrics Green/Sub-Green/Normal/Bad

AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) to set the indicator weight vectors W_B1, W_B2, W_B3 feeding the fuzzy aggregation; GDP-ratio weighting to roll 46 countries up to a 'world' membership vector and a scalar Score = 2*w1 + 1*w2 - 1*w4 in [-1,2]

Repeated (multi-period, one-year-cycle) game-theory model for spatial feasibility: opposing vs supporting vs neutral countries (classified by NRR), payoff functions F1-F4 combining a disaster loss curve (L_n = a^t vs Gaussian L_y), international-pressure term P = ln^alpha(t), and resource-proportional subsidy S = R_s*p; iterated until all opposing countries accept (within ~15-16 years)

Time-dimension 'environmental carrying capacity' benefit model: E_c = t*v*f - b, comparing remaining-resource value with vs without GGDP using fitted resource-use rate V_n = 12.0705t + 1299.892 plus change terms (logistic efficiency gain, welfare cost fs, treatment cost m); yields +$51.6T global income over 40 years

PVAR (Panel Vector AutoRegression) on Australia's 8 regions (1990-2020) with endogenous vars ATI/MTI/TTI (all/secondary/tertiary total factor income, log-transformed): panel unit-root tests (IPS, ADF-Fisher, PP-Fisher), AIC/BIC/HQIC lag selection, unit-circle stability check, GMM estimation, and variance decomposition showing tertiary contributes 47.27% vs secondary 21.07%
Comparative benchmarking of Australia vs Germany on waste (recycling/landfill/incineration rates) to justify waste-to-energy policy
key_techniques: Monetizing externalities: converting CO2 (via carbon market price MPC, inflation-adjusted), waste (via waste-to-energy kWh conversion and electricity price), and resource depletion (via NRR rents) into a single dollar deduction from GDP
Calibrating an abstract policy parameter (theta=3%) from real-world natural experiments rather than guessing it, lending empirical credibility

Quantile-based fuzzy membership: using the 2020 cross-country distribution (min + quartiles) as the grading yardstick so future-year scores are anchored to a concrete baseline rather than arbitrary thresholds
GDP-weighted aggregation from country level to world level, explicitly rejecting a naive mean because country sizes differ

Turning a yes/no policy question into a convergence result: the repeated game produces a concrete adoption timeline table (year-by-year R/A per country), making 'is it worthwhile' answerable with a date

Full econometric rigor on the country sub-model: stationarity tests, lag-order criteria, unit-circle stability, GMM, and variance decomposition — borrowing standard panel-data methodology to produce a defensible 47% vs 21% contrast
Closing the loop from quantitative findings to concrete, sequenced policy (e.g., the landfill 'three-step' phase-out and nature-based carbon sequestration), then to a plain-language government letter
why_it_won: "It is a complete, end-to-end policy narrative where every task feeds the next and every claim is reduced to a number a judge can check: a single RGG metric across 46 real countries, a 2040 forecast, a fuzzy climate score moving toward the ideal (2,2,2), a game that converges to universal adoption in ~15 years, a +$51.6T figure, and a 47.27% vs 21.07% PVAR result for Australia. The team didn't just assert GGDP is good — they answered the hardest sub-question (will countries actually agree?) with a repeated-game model that yields an explicit adoption timeline, which is exactly the kind of feasibility reasoning Problem F policy judges reward. The method portfolio is unusually broad and method-appropriate (fuzzy eval + AHP for multi-criteria climate, game theory for international equity, PVAR/GMM for industrial structure), and the country analysis carries real econometric rigor (unit-root, lag selection, variance decomposition) that most teams skip. The writing is judge-friendly: vivid framing, a clear summary tied to numbers, and a genuinely policy-styled government letter."
section_highlights: "Summary front-loads every headline number per task (RGG distribution, fuzzy scores before/after, 15-year game convergence, +$51.6T, 47.27%/21.07%) so a judge gets the whole result in one read. The 'Our Work' section + Figure 1 flowchart map the five-task dependency chain. Each task opens with an evocative section title ('GGDP — An environmental prism through which to view economy', 'It's time to make the right choice'). Assumptions are stated with explicit justifications. The PVAR section is laid out as a clean 7-step recipe (construction -> indicators -> stationarity -> lag order -> unit circle -> GMM -> variance decomposition), making a sophisticated method easy to follow. Sensitivity analysis on theta plus an honest Strengths/Weaknesses section. The closing one-page letter to the Australian Government translates all the math into executive, non-technical language as the problem demanded."
pitfalls_or_limitations: RGG values are implausibly high and compressed (mostly 0.95-1.00), meaning the GGDP deductions are tiny relative to GDP — the headline metric barely discriminates between countries and the 'world average rose from 0.9784 to 0.9881' change is economically trivial, undercutting the central narrative

The OCR/markdown rendering of the choropleth and scatter figures is badly corrupted (hundreds of duplicated 'Tanzania (Nigeria) 136,246' rows, placeholder scatter data), so the actual data visualizations cannot be verified from this file — a reminder the real PDF figures carry the evidence

Several game-theory and time-dimension functional forms are ad hoc and physically unmotivated: L_y as a Gaussian bump, P = ln^alpha(t), the efficiency change terms (logistic + sum of (sin i + 1)) look chosen for convenient curves rather than derived, weakening rigor

The pivotal theta=3% policy impact factor is justified by only two historical events and a single illustrative chart; sensitivity analysis varies it but the whole forecast hinges on this one weakly-grounded number
The fuzzy membership function in Eq.(5) contains an apparent typo/degeneracy (denominator q_i3 - q_i3 = 0), suggesting transcription or derivation errors in the affiliation formulas
Model assumes GGDP has negligible effect on GDP itself, which is a strong and arguably wrong simplification given the industrial transformation the paper later advocates
Self-acknowledged: ignores water, land, biodiversity in the climate analysis; forecast curves contain 'subjective elements'
Game payoffs are asserted to satisfy F1<F3, F2<F4 (everyone always better off under GGDP) almost by construction, so the 'game' is somewhat rigged toward the desired adoption conclusion
reusable_patterns: Five-task pipeline architecture for policy problems: (1) define & quantify the proposed measure on real multi-country data, (2) forecast its effect with a calibrated impact factor, (3) test feasibility/equity, (4) deep-dive one country with a rigorous sub-model, (5) translate to policy + stakeholder letter — each task's output is the next task's input
Calibrate abstract policy/behavior parameters from real historical natural experiments (crises, treaties) instead of asserting them, then sensitivity-test that parameter

For 'should we adopt X?' questions, model it as a repeated game across stakeholder classes and report a concrete convergence timeline table (round-by-round accept/reject) — far more persuasive than a static argument

Multi-criteria evaluation stack: AHP for weights + multi-level fuzzy comprehensive evaluation with quantile-anchored membership functions + size-weighted aggregation to a single interpretable score with a defined ideal point (e.g., (2,2,2))
Monetize-the-externality pattern: convert each non-market impact (emissions, waste, depletion) into dollars via a defensible market proxy so it can be subtracted from a headline economic figure

Full panel-econometrics recipe as a drop-in country/industry sub-model: PVAR + unit-root tests + AIC/BIC lag selection + unit-circle stability + GMM + variance decomposition to rank drivers (use when you have panel data over regions/years)
Benchmark the focal country against a well-known peer (Australia vs Germany) to make gaps concrete and motivate borrowed best-practice policies
Summary-as-scoreboard: lead each task's summary line with its single most important number so judges absorb all results before reading the body
